title: PRBench: Large-Scale Expert Rubrics for Evaluating High-Stakes Professional Reasoning
human_time: ~30-90 min per task: open-ended legal/finance analysis a JD/CFA would draft against ~18-criterion rubrics; estimated, paper doesn't state.
frontier_score: GPT-5 / GPT-5 Pro lead; best 0.39 (Finance) and 0.37 (Legal) on Hard subsets, 0.51/0.50 on full set
one_line: 1,100 expert-authored legal & finance tasks scored against 19,356 weighted rubric criteria by an LLM judge.
notes: ~30% of tasks carry multi-turn context but the model only writes the final response, so classified single-turn. Headline eval is tool-free (web-search/code-interpreter runs are ablations, all <=0.40).

title: VQA: Visual Question Answering
human_time: Seconds per item: one short question about one image; 89% of answers are a single word and humans hit 83.3% at a glance.
frontier_score: Best 2015 model 58.16% open-ended / 63.09% multiple-choice (test-standard) vs 83.30% human
one_line: Free-form questions about images graded by consensus string match against 10 human answers; ~0.76M questions on COCO.
notes: Saturation judged from own knowledge: v1 was retired for balanced VQA v2 and modern multimodal models exceed the 2015 human-model gap. items_count is total questions; eval test splits are a subset. Held-out test-standard/test-reserve eval server = private-holdout.

title: HealthBench: Evaluating Large Language Models Towards Improved Human Health
human_time: Minutes per item: read a ~2.6-turn health chat and compose one thorough reply; physicians wrote their own responses in-session.
frontier_score: o3: 0.60 overall; 0.32 on HealthBench Hard
one_line: 5,000 open-ended health conversations graded against 48,562 physician-written rubric criteria by a validated GPT-4.1 judge.
notes: Provenance borderline: physicians authored the rubrics and scenario specs but an LM pipeline generated most dialogues (synthetic-generated is defensible). Single-turn since the model only answers the final user message. 0.60 sits right at the open/closing boundary; Hard subset (0.32) is open. Canary string also shipped, but private holdout is the stronger defense.

title: MCP-Atlas: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Tool-Use Competency with Real MCP Servers
human_time: ~10-30 min per task for a human: multi-server lookup/orchestration workflows (mean 9.8 tool calls, 2.55 servers); estimated from task descriptions.
frontier_score: Muse Spark (Meta) 82.2% Pass@0.75, mean coverage 86.2%; open GLM-5.1 at 75.6%
one_line: 1,000 agentic tasks over 36 live MCP servers and 220 real tools, scored by an answer-centric claims rubric per task.
notes: tool_access lists code-exec and search-retrieval because the MCP server pool includes code executors/cli and search servers, all exposed via MCP. Prompts are single-turn but execution is a full agent loop, so interactive-env. 500/500 public/private split = private-holdout.

title: A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference
human_time: Seconds per pair: read two short sentences (14 and 8 tokens on average) and pick entailment/contradiction/neutral.
frontier_score: Best 2015 models: 78.2% (lexicalized feature classifier), 77.6% (100d LSTM); modern models >90%
one_line: 570K crowd-written sentence pairs labeled entailment/contradiction/neutral - the corpus that made neural NLI trainable.
notes: items_count is the 10K test split of the 570,152-pair corpus. Saturation partly from own knowledge (modern models >91%; community treats SNLI as solved and artifact-ridden). Reasoning secondary since entailment is inference, though rubric files NLI under language.

title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
human_time: Seconds per item: one frontal portrait, one binary gender label.
frontier_score: Overall accuracy 87.9-93.7% (Microsoft best at 93.7%), but darker-female error up to 34.7% (IBM)
one_line: Skin-type-and-gender-balanced face benchmark (PPB) exposing intersectional error gaps in commercial gender classifiers.
notes: Audit paper; classified artifact is the Pilot Parliaments Benchmark. Fairness/bias auditing has no taxonomy bucket, so 'safety' secondary is an imperfect fit. Provenance: images harvested from official parliament portraits (real-work-mined) with expert dermatologist labeling. Saturated per the 2019 follow-up showing vendors patched the gaps; the audit is treated as historical.

title: LexGLUE: A Benchmark Dataset for Legal Language Understanding in English
human_time: Most items are per-provision/per-sentence classification or a 5-way CaseHOLD MCQ (~1-10 min); long ECtHR/SCOTUS judgment-prediction cases push toward 30+ min but are the minority of items.
frontier_score: Legal-BERT leads the harmonic-mean aggregate at 70.8 macro-F1 / 78.9 micro-F1 (UNFAIR-ToS ~96 micro-F1, LEDGAR ~88)
one_line: GLUE-for-law: seven English legal-NLU classification/QA datasets unified behind one micro/macro-F1 leaderboard.
notes: Borderline domain: tasks are classic NLU classification (language) but over legal expert material - filed as professional (law) per the LegalBench precedent, language secondary. items_count is the approximate combined test splits (~23.9K); saturation judged in the fine-tuning era the explainer reports.

title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
human_time: Paper's own complexity annotation for an ML engineer today: 30% Low (<2 h), 50% Medium (2-10 h), 20% High (>10 h) - typical instance is several hours.
frontier_score: o1-preview + AIDE earns a medal in 16.9% of competitions (pass@1), 34.1% at pass@8
one_line: 75 hand-ported Kaggle competitions where agents do end-to-end ML engineering, medal-graded against real human leaderboards.
notes: Contamination: the paper runs honest probes (familiarity r=-0.24, obfuscation 8.5->8.4) but ships no structural defense - all data is public Kaggle, so none-public. Anti-cheat (Dolos plagiarism, log analyzer) is rule enforcement, not contamination defense.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
human_time: Each item is one self-contained function from a docstring at easy software-interview difficulty - a few minutes for a competent programmer.
frontier_score: Codex-S 12B: 37.7% pass@1, 77.5% oracle pass@100 (paper-era); community now treats HumanEval as solved
one_line: HumanEval: 164 hand-written Python problems graded by executing hidden unit tests; origin of pass@k and execution grading.
notes: Problems were hand-written to avoid training overlap - creation-time hygiene, but the set shipped fully public with no holdout, so none-public (explainer notes it later leaked into corpora). Saturation call uses community consensus beyond the file's 2021 numbers.

title: FinQA: A Dataset of Numerical Reasoning over Financial Data
human_time: One question over a single filing page with a <=5-step arithmetic chain; experts wrote ~2 questions/page at ~$35/hr (~3-4 min/question), so answering is a few minutes for an analyst.
frontier_score: FinQANet (RoBERTa-large): 61.24% execution accuracy vs 91.16% for human finance experts
one_line: 8,281 expert-written numerical-reasoning questions over real S&P 500 filings, each with a gold executable arithmetic program.
notes: Domain judged by the work's nature (financial-analyst reasoning) -> professional, math secondary. items_count is the test split (1,147 of 8,281 total). Headline execution accuracy is numeric exact-match; program accuracy adds a symbolic-equivalence programmatic check. Saturation: explainer says modern LLMs 'largely closed' the 30-point gap but quotes no modern number - closing, from own knowledge.

title: Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference
human_time: A typical battle is a short open-ended chat prompt (~95 tokens, avg 1.3 turns) - answering well takes a human a few minutes.
one_line: Live crowdsourced arena: users prompt two anonymous models, vote the winner; Bradley-Terry turns ~240K votes into an Elo leaderboard.
notes: Task shape: real-user dialogue averaging 1.3 turns - mostly single-turn in practice but multi-turn by design. No fixed item set (243,329 conversations at the paper's snapshot), so items_count null; relative leaderboard with no ceiling -> saturation not-applicable.

title: MoReBench: Evaluating Procedural and Pluralistic Moral Reasoning in Language Models, More than Outcomes
human_time: One 44-393-word dilemma answered with a thorough reasoned trace hitting ~23 criteria - roughly 15-30 min for an expert (validation experts wrote such traces by hand).
frontier_score: Models satisfy 72.0-85.5% of harmlessness criteria (best 85.5%, GPT-5-mini) but only up to 65.1% of logical-process criteria (Qwen3-235B)
one_line: 1,000 moral dilemmas graded on the reasoning process via 23,018 philosopher-written weighted criteria scored by an LLM judge.
notes: Secondary safety: 41.4% of scenarios are AI-safety-flavored Moral Agent dilemmas and harmlessness is a scored dimension. A 150-scenario MoReBench-Theory sibling is excluded from items_count. Saturation is a judgment call from per-dimension 48-85% satisfaction rates - roughly 'closing' but the metric has no clean ceiling.

title: Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems
human_time: Grade-school word problems of 2-8 steps; a competent adult solves one in roughly 1-5 minutes (the rubric's own L2 exemplar).
frontier_score: frontier models exceeded 95% within a couple of years (paper-era best: 175B verifier ~55% vs ~33% finetuned)
one_line: 8.5K human-written grade-school math word problems (1K test) graded by final-answer match; the default math-reasoning yardstick.
notes: Provenance from own knowledge: problems written by contracted Surge AI workers (quality-controlled crowdworkers), not domain experts; explainer only says 'human-written'. The paper's own eval injects calculator annotations that override arithmetic at test time, but standard benchmark use is pure text-in/text-out, so tool_access=none.

title: OR-Bench: An Over-Refusal Benchmark for Large Language Models
human_time: One borderline prompt, one answer-or-refuse decision; the graded refusal judgment takes a human seconds (a full helpful answer might take a minute or two).
frontier_score: Llama-3.1-70B 3.0% over-refusal on Hard-1K (lowest in study); GPT-4o 6.7%; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 43.8%
one_line: 80K auto-generated borderline-benign prompts (plus Hard-1K and ~600 toxic) measuring over-refusal vs safety across models.
notes: Grading: headline Hard-1K/Toxic numbers use a GPT-4 refusal judge (no per-item rubric -> llm-judge); the full 80K uses keyword refusal matching, which I coded as programmatic. Contamination_defense=procedural is borderline: the released 80K is a static public set, but the fully automated Mixtral+ensemble pipeline is shipped and pitched as re-runnable to regenerate fresh borderline prompts. Saturation 'closing': best models sit within a few points of the ~1.8% label-noise floor on over-refusal, but the joint safe-AND-helpful objective is unsolved (Spearman 0.89 trade-off) and the Claude family still refuses heavily.

title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents
human_time: Typical user task is an office chore (summarize emails and forward, make a bank transfer, book travel) a human finishes in a few minutes with the same tools.
frontier_score: best agents hijacked in <25% of cases under the strongest generic attack (~8% with a detector); benign utility <66%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet best trade-off
one_line: Stateful gym of 70 tools/97 tasks/629 injection cases testing tool-using agents against indirect prompt injection, scored by Python state checks.
notes: Environments and tasks hand-built by the ETH authors (dummy data LLM-generated then manually inspected) -> expert-authored. Contamination defense: fully synthetic data and an extensible attack/defense framework mitigate staleness, but nothing fits private-holdout/live-refresh, so none-public. Saturation 'open' reads the security+utility joint objective; ASR alone has no clean ceiling.

title: ImageNet: A Large-Scale Hierarchical Image Database
human_time: Recognizing the object category in one image takes a human seconds (fine-grained synsets like Burmese cat a bit longer).
one_line: 3.2M web-crawled, MTurk-verified images over 5,247 WordNet synsets; the dataset whose ILSVRC descendant defined deep-learning-era vision.
notes: The 2009 paper is a construction report with no test split or model baselines; I classified the artifact it ships (the labeled database, consumed as label-match classification, per the downstream ILSVRC protocol). Explainer quotes no model scores (99.7% is labeling precision), so frontier_score=null, but saturation=saturated from own knowledge: ILSVRC surpassed human top-5 error and retired in 2017. items_count is the database size at publication, not a fixed eval set.

title: Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web
human_time: Tasks average 7.3 actions (book a flight leg, filter Netflix comedies, like a tweet) - a few minutes each for a human web user.
frontier_score: best step SR 52.0% (MindAct Flan-T5XL, Cross-Task); whole-task success <=7.1%, GPT-4 2.0%
one_line: 2,350 crowd-annotated high-level tasks on 137 real websites, graded on cross-task/-website/-domain generalization of web agents.
notes: task_shape is borderline: conceptually a web-agent action loop (so interactive-env), but the official eval replays cached snapshots teacher-forced with oracle history - the environment never reacts to the model's own actions. grading_all includes proxy-metric for Operation F1 (token-F1 on typed values). items_count = 1,341 test tasks across the three splits (2,350 total incl. 1,009 train). Saturation judged from the explainer's 2023 numbers; later multimodal agents score higher but end-to-end whole-task success remained far from ceiling.

title: SWE-Bench Pro: Can AI Agents Solve Long-Horizon Software Engineering Tasks?
human_time: Multi-file enterprise changes averaging 107 LOC across 4.1 files; the paper says a professional takes hours to days - typical instance sits in the hours band.
frontier_score: Claude Sonnet 4.5 43.6% Pass@1 on the public set; best on private commercial repos Opus 4.1 17.8%
one_line: 1,865 human-verified multi-file issue-resolution tasks from copyleft and purchased commercial repos, hardening SWE-bench against saturation and contamination.
notes: Tasks mined from real commit histories (plus purchased startup codebases) with heavy human augmentation of problem statements/requirements/interfaces - primary provenance stays real-work-mined. Strongest shipped defense is the 858-task private held-out set plus the 276 never-public commercial set (private-holdout); the public tier's copyleft argument is legal, not empirical. An LLM judge is used only for failure analysis, not scoring.

title: TRAIL: Trace Reasoning and Agentic Issue Localization
human_time: Paper's own annotation numbers: 30-40 minutes per trace ($12.66) for expert annotators to find/classify errors in one trace.
frontier_score: Gemini-2.5-Pro: 11% joint accuracy (18% GAIA / 5% SWE-Bench)
one_line: 148 expert-annotated OpenTelemetry agent traces; LLM judges must classify and locate 841 errors inside multi-million-token structured logs.
notes: Traces are freshly generated by running agents (organic induced errors) then expert-annotated - provenance called expert-authored on the gold labels, but arguably synthetic-generated on the trace side. Freshness is incidental, not a shipped defense, hence none-public. Long-context is co-primary in practice (scores anti-correlate with input length; 3/8 models exceed context).

title: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents
human_time: Paper frames deep-research agents as compressing 'hours of desk research into minutes'; each PhD-level task yields an analyst-grade cited report - several hours for a human expert.
frontier_score: Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research: RACE Overall 48.88 (111.21 effective citations; Perplexity DR leads citation accuracy at 90.24%)
one_line: 100 expert-written PhD-level deep-research tasks (50 EN/50 ZH); web-research agents graded on report quality (RACE) and citation trustworthiness (FACT).
notes: RACE uses judge-generated adaptive criteria with dynamic weights against a reference report - classified rubric-judge; FACT's per-claim binary support verdicts are llm-judge. Scores are relative to a Gemini-written reference (0.5 = parity), so saturation is not-applicable: read rankings, not a ceiling. Task mix grounded in 44,019 real user queries but items themselves are expert-authored.

title: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
human_time: 805 short single-turn chat instructions (self-instruct/Vicuna/Koala style) - a few minutes each for a human to answer well.
frontier_score: GPT-4: 79.0% simulated / 69.8% human win-rate vs Davinci-003
one_line: Simulated-annotator RLHF sandbox whose eval artifact is an 805-prompt pairwise win-rate benchmark vs Davinci-003, judged by a noisy 13-LLM pool.
notes: Eval-methodology paper - classified the shipped artifact (805-prompt win-rate eval + simulated-annotator pool, the AlpacaEval precursor). Prompts are recombined from prior public instruction sets, hence aggregated. Human grading appears in the validation loop (Mhuman), not the headline metric. Win-rate vs the fixed Davinci-003 reference is effectively saturated/superseded (length-controlled AlpacaEval replaced it).

title: HiL-Bench (Human-in-Loop Benchmark): Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help?
human_time: 150 SWE-Bench Pro repo issues (hours each for an engineer) + 150 BIRD SQL tasks (tens of minutes); typical instance ~1 hour including blocker clarification.
frontier_score: GPT-5.5: 29% combined pass@3, 53% Ask-F1
one_line: 300 SWE/SQL tasks seeded with hidden blockers; measures whether agents know when to call ask_human(), via spam-proof Ask-F1 plus pass@3.
notes: Base tasks come from SWE-Bench Pro/BIRD but the tested content (1,131 expert-injected, multiply-audited blockers) is expert-authored - aggregated is the runner-up call. Grading: pass@3 is programmatic (tests/execution); Ask-F1 relevance is adjudicated by a frozen Llama-3.3-70B semantic judge (97/91 validated), hence llm-judge in grading_all. Secondary 'reasoning' covers the metacognitive uncertainty-detection skill the benchmark isolates. 200 public / 100 private held-out tasks.

title: MultiNRC: A Challenging and Native Multilingual Reasoning Evaluation Benchmark for LLMs
human_time: Each item is a self-contained riddle/grammar/cultural-math question with a word-or-phrase answer - a few minutes for a native-speaker expert.
frontier_score: o3-pro (high): 49.00% - no model clears 50%
one_line: 1,055 natively-authored (not translated) French/Spanish/Chinese reasoning questions across linguistic, wordplay, cultural, and cultural-math categories.
notes: Grading call: answers are short objective gold strings, but equivalence is checked by a GPT-4.1 judge (>95% human agreement, Scott's Pi 0.88) - classified exact-match in spirit with llm-judge as the matching mechanism. Adversarial 3-of-5-models-fail filter builds in the low ceiling and is not a contamination defense; set is public on Hugging Face.

title: MCP-RADAR: A Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Evaluating Tool Use Capabilities in Large Language Models
human_time: Tasks are single operations or short chains (send an email, file op, terminal command, tool-assisted math/web lookup) - minutes each for a human with the same tools.
frontier_score: Gemini 2.5 Pro best overall (Email 82.5%, Terminal 59.9%, Web 29.8%; ~57% cross-domain average)
one_line: 507 MCP tool-use tasks across six domains (math, web, email, calendar, files, terminal), graded by exact-match answers/operations plus turn-level call validity.
notes: Provenance split: ~300 Fuzzy tasks are template-generated per tool (synthetic primary); the 214 math/web Precise tasks are adapted from prior academic datasets (aggregated minority). The Gemini-Flash 'answerable without tools' filter targets memorization but isn't one of the listed contamination defenses. Saturation borderline: email ~82% closing but web <30% and best cross-domain average sits under 60% - called open.

title: RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code
human_time: ~5 min/task: the paper's own human baseline gave a proficient developer 5 minutes per task with an IDE (solved 87%).
frontier_score: SWE-agent + Claude-3.5-Sonnet (descriptive) 35%; state-aware GPT-4 agent 38/100 vs human 87%
one_line: 100 handcrafted multi-file Python refactoring tasks probing coding agents' stateful reasoning, graded by AST unit tests.
notes: Reference solutions are withheld (only AST tests released), but tasks live in public repos and the task set is public, so contamination_defense = none-public. Paper notes reasoning-model agents already solve 'many more' tasks, so 'open' reflects the quoted 2024-era panel.

title: Audio MultiChallenge: A Multi-Turn Evaluation of Spoken Dialogue Systems on Natural Human Interaction
human_time: A few minutes: listen to a 3-8-turn conversation (~2 min of audio on average) and compose the final-turn response satisfying its ~3.8 rubrics.
frontier_score: Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking): 54.65% APR (82.54% ARS)
one_line: 452 unscripted multi-turn spoken dialogues with 1,712 atomic rubrics testing voice models on natural human conversation.
notes: Provenance is borderline: tasks are recorded by vetted Scale AI human contributors guided by agentic blueprints (65% blueprint-leveraged) with multi-stage QC — could arguably be crowdsourced. Fresh Dec-2025 original recordings give low leakage risk, but no formal holdout mechanism is described, hence none-public. Grading is fixed-context on the final turn, transcript-only.

title: CRUXEval: A Benchmark for Code Reasoning, Understanding and Execution
human_time: ~1 minute: the filter explicitly keeps only problems 'a good programmer could solve in about a minute' without scratch memory.
frontier_score: GPT-4 + CoT: 74.8% (input) / 81.9% (output) pass@1
one_line: 800 short Python functions; predict output from input (CRUXEval-O) or input from output (CRUXEval-I), execution-checked.
notes: Complexity sits at the L1/L2 boundary (~1 min by design). The generate-and-filter recipe is re-runnable for harder future versions, but the shipped set is fixed and public, so not 'procedural'. Explainer notes the 2023 panel is dated and the ceiling is almost certainly higher now, hence 'closing'.

title: LegalBench: A Collaboratively Built Benchmark for Measuring Legal Reasoning in Large Language Models
human_time: Well under a minute typical: most items are short binary/classification questions on a clause or brief fact pattern; rule-application explanations take a lawyer a few minutes.
frontier_score: GPT-4 (balanced-acc): rule-conclusion 89.9, issue-spotting 82.9, rule-recall 59.2
one_line: 162 legal-reasoning tasks hand-built by 40 legal professionals, organized by the IRAC framework, scored by balanced accuracy.
notes: 162 tasks across 36 corpora; total instance count not stated, so items_count = null. Grading is mostly exact-match balanced accuracy, with multi-label F1 (proxy-metric) and manual human grading of rule-application 'analysis' by one law-trained grader. Leakage openly acknowledged (Appendix D maps prior web availability) but no defense shipped. Saturation mixed by category (89.9 conclusion vs 59.2 rule-recall) — 'closing' on balance.

title: Beyond Seeing: Evaluating MLLMs on Tool-Enabled Image Perception, Transformation, and Reasoning
human_time: Minutes: human reference solutions need fewer than five tool calls — inspect/crop/enhance an image, maybe compute or search, then answer.
frontier_score: GPT-5-think: 18.68% APR (0.4712 ARS)
one_line: 1,204 expert tasks where MLLMs must crop/edit/enhance images with tools to answer, graded on 7,777 weighted rubrics.
notes: Tool set: python_image_processing + python_interpreter (code-exec), web_search + browser_get_page_text (search-retrieval; text fetch, not interactive browsing), calculator + historical_weather (domain-api). Original contributor images and a >=2-of-3-models-fail admission filter lower leakage, but no holdout mechanism is described. The 18.68% floor is partly a selection artifact (only tasks that broke 2 of 3 reference models are admitted).

title: OlympiadBench: A Challenging Benchmark for Promoting AGI with Olympiad-Level Bilingual Multimodal Scientific Problems
human_time: ~10-40 min for a strong human on a typical competition/Gaokao item; hardest IMO/IPhO problems run to hours.
frontier_score: GPT-4V: 17.97% avg (21.70 math / 10.74 physics) on the 2024 panel
one_line: 8,476 olympiad-level bilingual multimodal math/physics free-response problems with automatic answer-equivalence grading.
notes: Grading straddles exact-match and programmatic: final-answer equivalence via SymPy symbolic checking + numeric tolerance; the 21% theorem-proving slice has no auto-grader (manual sampling only, hence 'human' in grading_all). Quoted 2024 numbers are open (17.97%), but the explainer says later work treats it as a saturating sub-benchmark under reasoning models — 'closing' reflects the current frontier. PDF-to-markdown sourcing is best-effort leakage mitigation, not a shipped defense.

title: WebVoyager: Building an End-to-End Web Agent with Large Multimodal Models
human_time: ~2-5 min per task for a human (find/read info on a public site); agent capped at 15 steps.
frontier_score: WebVoyager (GPT-4V backbone) 59.1% task success on 643 tasks, human eval
one_line: Live-web agent benchmark: 643 open-ended tasks on 15 real sites, scored by human/GPT-4V trajectory judgment.
notes: Tasks are GPT-4 self-instruct generated, human-verified -> synthetic-generated. Live-web drift is incidental, tasks fixed/public -> none-public. Saturation from outside knowledge (later agents report ~87-90%+ and treat it as solved, though 2025 audits dispute judge inflation); explainer quotes only the 2024 59.1%.

title: AdvancedIF: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and Reinforcement Learning for Advancing LLM Instruction Following
human_time: ~10-30 min to write one response satisfying 6-20 layered constraints over the given conversation.
frontier_score: GPT-5 (full thinking) 77.9 overall; Gemini 3 Pro 74.7
one_line: 1,645 expert-written prompts with human rubrics testing complex, multi-turn, and system-prompt instruction following.
notes: Task shape borderline: 1,243 of 1,645 items are multi-turn dialogs, but the context is fixed and only the final-turn response is graded -> classed single-turn. Set was unreleased at publication (release promised), so no shipped defense -> none-public.

title: Aligning AI With Shared Human Values
human_time: Seconds to ~1 min: read a short scenario (occasionally 1-6 paragraphs) and give a binary/comparative moral judgment.
frontier_score: ALBERT-xxlarge (fine-tuned) 71.0% Test / 47.9% Hard Test (2020)
one_line: 130K+ everyday scenarios testing whether models predict consensus moral judgments across five ethical frameworks.
notes: Provenance mixed: qualification-gated MTurkers wrote most tasks, long commonsense items scraped from Reddit -> crowdsourced. Utilitarianism's pairwise ranking still scores against fixed labels -> exact-match. Saturated per the explainer's own critique (modern models near-ceiling zero-shot, contaminated since 2020).

title: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding
human_time: Under a minute per 4-option exam question for a knowledgeable human; hardest professional items a few minutes.
frontier_score: GPT-3 175B few-shot 43.9% avg (2020, best in paper)
one_line: 57-subject four-option multiple-choice exam of pretraining world knowledge, from elementary to professional level.
notes: items_count (14,042 test questions) from outside knowledge, not stated in the explainer. Saturation from outside knowledge: frontier models score ~88-90%+ and the community treats MMLU as retired (MMLU-Pro/GPQA built to escape it); the explainer quotes only 2020 numbers.

title: Measuring Coding Challenge Competence With APPS
human_time: Typical interview-tier problem ~20-30 min for a competent programmer; competition tier can take 1+ hours.
frontier_score: GPT-Neo 2.7B (fine-tuned) 3.90% strict acc Introductory (2021); cited Codex 12B 25% @1000 samples
one_line: 10,000 coding-challenge problems in three difficulty tiers, graded by executing Python against hidden test cases.
notes: Complexity sits on the L2/L3 line (competition tier is hours; typical interview item ~20-30 min). BLEU is reported only to debunk it, so not in grading_all. Saturated call is outside knowledge: community retired APPS (contamination + supersession by LiveCodeBench/SWE-bench; frontier reasoning models handle competition-level problems); the explainer's own 2021 numbers are near zero. items_count = 5,000 test problems of 10,000 total.

title: CUAD: An Expert-Annotated NLP Dataset for Legal Contract Review
human_time: At 5-10 min/page and contracts up to 150 pages, finding one category's clauses in a contract runs ~30 min to hours.
frontier_score: DeBERTa-xlarge 44.0% Precision @ 80% Recall (47.8 AUPR), 2021
one_line: 510 SEC contracts with 13,101 expert-labeled clauses across 41 categories; extractive span-finding for legal review.
notes: Grading: Jaccard>=0.5 word-overlap span match feeding AUPR/P@R is token-overlap -> proxy-metric, not exact-match. Provenance: real EDGAR contracts, expert-annotated (expert-authored is arguable). items_count is annotated clauses, not (contract x label) test queries (~4.2K). 'Open' rests on 2021 encoder numbers; no modern-LLM results quoted and the benchmark is little-tracked now.

title: Measuring Mathematical Problem Solving With the MATH Dataset
human_time: Paper's own human study: 20 problems in 1 hour (no calculator), i.e. ~3 min per problem for strong humans; competition items typically take a few minutes each.
frontier_score: o1-/R1-class systems report 90%+ on the MATH test set (2024-25); paper-era best was GPT-2 1.5B at 6.9%
one_line: 12,500 competition-math problems (5,000 test) with worked solutions, graded by exact match on a single boxed final answer.
notes: items_count is the 5,000-problem test set (12,500 total incl. train). Explainer explicitly names contamination as the dominant caveat: public AMC/AIME problems mirrored on AoPS, no defense shipped.

title: MLAgentBench: Evaluating Language Agents on Machine Learning Experimentation
human_time: Improving a starter ML model by 10% on a real dataset (edit code, train, iterate) is a multi-hour job for an ML engineer; agents were capped at 50 actions / 5 hours per run.
frontier_score: Claude 3 Opus agent: 37.5% average success rate (100% on house-price, 0% on BabyLM/parkinsons/fathomnet/vectorization)
one_line: 13 ML-experimentation tasks where an LM agent reads, edits, and executes training code to beat a starter baseline by 10%.
notes: Provenance is a mix: most tasks reframe prior benchmark datasets (CIFAR-10, IMDb, ogbn-arxiv, CLRS, BabyLM) plus real Kaggle challenges, so 'aggregated' over 'real-work-mined' is a judgment call. Contamination defense is post-cutoff Kaggle sourcing by design, but only for 4 of 13 tasks and not continuously refreshed.

title: MultiLoKo: a multilingual local knowledge benchmark for LLMs spanning 31 languages
human_time: Closed-form short-answer factoid questions from popular Wikipedia pages; a native speaker who knows the fact answers in seconds.
frontier_score: Gemini 2.0 Flash 34.4 average EM (dev), Llama 3.1 405B 34.3, GPT-4o 34.0 -- even the easiest languages cap below 50 EM
one_line: 31-language benchmark of 500 locally-sourced Wikipedia knowledge questions per language, made fully parallel via translation.
notes: Provenance is borderline: recruited native-speaker annotators with multi-pass QC (SQuAD-style) -- professional annotators rather than domain experts, so crowdsourced over expert-authored. Secondary 'language' because the design explicitly separates language proficiency from knowledge. Blind frequency-split test set behind an external submission service = private-holdout.

title: FinanceBench: A New Benchmark for Financial Question Answering
human_time: A financial analyst locating a figure in a 10-K/10-Q and computing a metric from it: roughly 5-15 minutes per question.
frontier_score: GPT-4-Turbo: 19% correct on the realistic shared vector store; 79% in long-context; 85% with oracle evidence
one_line: Open-book financial QA: expert (question, answer, evidence) triplets over 361 real SEC filings, graded by human review.
notes: Tool access varies by configuration (closed-book none, vector-store retrieval, long-context, oracle); the headline realistic setup is RAG retrieval. Only 150 of 10,231 cases are public (and all graded numbers come from those 150). Saturation call is borderline: realistic-RAG headline is 19% (open) but best realistic long-context config hits 79%, so 'closing' on late-2023 models.

title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code
human_time: LeetCode/AtCoder contest problems bucketed Easy/Medium/Hard; a competent competitive programmer solves a typical one in ~10-30 minutes.
frontier_score: GPT-4-Turbo 39.7 Pass@1 on LCB-Easy code generation (paper snapshot, early 2024)
one_line: Live, release-date-stamped contest-coding benchmark grading generation, self-repair, code execution, and test-output prediction.
notes: Self-repair supplies execution feedback in the prompt but there is no agentic loop, so single-turn with no tool access. Execution/test-output-prediction scenarios grade by value equality (exact-match); headline is unit-test execution. 511 problems total, 349 in the contamination-safe main comparison. The canonical live-refresh benchmark. Saturation judged from the explainer's 2024 numbers; later reasoning models score much higher on newer rolling windows (own knowledge, not from text).

title: Critical Foreign Policy Decisions (CFPD)-Benchmark: Measuring Diplomatic Preferences in Large Language Models
human_time: Reading a paragraph-length IR dilemma with deliberately balanced options and choosing a course of action: a minute or two per item for an IR-literate reader.
one_line: Measures foreign-policy dispositions, not correctness: 400 expert IR scenarios expanded to 66k forced-choice questions with no answer key.
notes: No ground truth by design, so several dimensions are judgment calls: domain_primary 'social-moral' (value/political preference probe) with 'safety' secondary for escalation propensity; grading is forced-choice extraction (guided decoding for open models = exact-match; closed-model free text manually encoded = human); saturation not-applicable since the output is a preference distribution. items_count is the 66,473 expanded questions (400 base scenarios). Dataset withheld -- private by design.

title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
human_time: Gold patches average 1.7 files / 32.8 lines in 438K-line repos with multi-point edits; a competent engineer needs roughly 1-4 hours per issue.
frontier_score: Claude 3 Opus 3.79% (BM25, 2023); agent scaffolds pushed models past 40-50% within a year
one_line: 2,294 real GitHub issues: patch a Python repo so its hidden fail-to-pass unit tests pass — execution-graded SWE.
notes: Task_shape/tool_access reflect canonical agentic use; the paper's own 2023 baselines were single-turn retrieval-augmented generation with no tools. Contamination: date-split analysis is a probe and 'continually refreshable' a design claim, but the shipped 2,294 set is fully public. Saturation call leans on the explainer's 40-50% scaffold numbers plus own knowledge (SWE-bench Verified now ~70%+).

title: FORTRESS: Frontier Risk Evaluation for National Security and Public Safety
human_time: One single-turn adversarial (or benign-twin) prompt -> one response; reading and answering/refusing takes a few minutes.
frontier_score: Claude 3.5 Sonnet most robust: ARS 14.09 (but ORS 21.80); o3 balanced at ARS 17.20 / ORS 7.80
one_line: 500 expert adversarial NSPS prompts + benign twins; rubric-panel-graded risk (ARS) vs over-refusal (ORS) on CBRNE/terror/crime.
notes: items_count = 500 public adversarial prompts, each with a benign twin (1,010 adversarial total; ~510 held private as the contamination holdout). ARS uses per-item 4-7-question rubrics voted by a 3-judge panel (rubric-judge); ORS uses a single holistic GPT-4o-mini judge (llm-judge). 'Open': best models remain far from ideal ARS~0/ORS~0 and the two trade off.

title: ContractNLI: A Dataset for Document-level Natural Language Inference for Contracts
human_time: One item = read a full NDA (~2,254 tokens avg) and judge one policy hypothesis plus mark evidence spans — several minutes.
frontier_score: DeBERTa-xlarge + CUAD transfer: NLI acc .892, evidence mAP .936 (contradiction F1 only .405)
one_line: 607 NDAs x 17 fixed policy hypotheses: document-level entail/contradict/not-mentioned plus evidence-span identification.
notes: Professional-vs-language primary is borderline: it is classic NLI machinery, but the work is real legal contract review over real NDAs, so classified by the work's nature. items_count = 607 docs x 17 hypotheses (~10.3K pairs; test = 123 docs, ~2.1K pairs). Evidence mAP treated as exact-match against gold span sets. Saturation: headline acc .892 reads closing, but the hard contradiction/negation-by-exception subclass is wide open.

title: Refusal-Trained LLMs Are Easily Jailbroken As Browser Agents
human_time: Each behavior is a short browser task (send an email, register an account, post content) — a human completes one in a few minutes; agents get a 10-step cap.
frontier_score: GPT-4o agent: 74% ASR direct-ask, 100% under attack ensemble; most robust agent (Claude 3 Opus) still 41%
one_line: 100 harmful browser-agent behaviors on synthetic + live sites testing whether chat refusal training survives agentic framing.
notes: ASR judged by a GPT-4o classifier (HarmBench-style, holistic — no per-item rubric); synthetic-site action listeners log actions but do not grade. Provenance mixed: 46 original red-team-authored, 54 adapted from HarmBench/AIR-Bench (partly aggregated). 'Open' read from the defender's side: even the most robust agent is jailbroken 41% of the time under ensemble attack.

title: Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research
human_time: One item = find (or decline to find) a long+short answer in a full Wikipedia page for one real query — roughly 1-3 minutes of scanning.
frontier_score: Paper-era best: 55.0 long-answer F1 vs single human 73.4 and super-annotator ceiling 87.2
one_line: 307K real Google queries paired with full Wikipedia pages; extract long + short answers or abstain, scored vs a human ceiling.
notes: Language-vs-knowledge primary is borderline: real info-seeking world-knowledge queries, but the shipped task is doc-grounded extraction in the SQuAD lineage -> language, with knowledge and long-context (full-page input, retrieval lineage: DPR/BEIR) secondary. Provenance: queries mined from real search logs (user-traffic) with crowd annotation. Saturated call uses post-paper knowledge (BERT-era+ models approached the 87.2 ceiling; community treats the original task as retired) — the explainer itself only quotes pre-BERT 55 F1. items_count = test split (7,842); 307K train + 7.8K dev.

title: DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation
human_time: Problems average 140 words with 3.6-line reference solutions; a fluent data scientist solves one in a few minutes.
frontier_score: Codex-002 (Insertion) 43.3% pass@1 — 2022 best, since 'cleared comfortably' by modern models per the explainer
one_line: 1,000 StackOverflow data-science problems across 7 Python libraries, execution-graded and perturbed to defeat memorization.
notes: Contamination: ships a genuine one-time perturbation defense (>half of problems modified from their StackOverflow sources) that doesn't fit the taxonomy buckets — the set is static and public with no holdout/refresh, so none-public with this caveat. Grading combines execution tests and AST surface-form constraints, both programmatic. Saturation uses own knowledge that modern models score well above 43.3%.

title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
human_time: A few minutes per typical item (read a figure, compute a sequence task, retrieve a finding); the 41 CloningScenarios take a biologist 10-60 min but are the tail, not the median.
frontier_score: models cluster at low absolute accuracy (~0.2-0.35); Claude 3.5 Sonnet only matches/edges humans on TableQA
one_line: 2,457-question multiple-choice benchmark of practical biology-research tasks: lit search, figure/table reading, DB lookup, protocol troubleshooting, sequence/cloning.
notes: domain_primary=science per rubric (LAB-Bench named there); multimodal secondary because FigQA/TableQA (~531 items) are caption-less images. Provenance is a split: LitQA/SuppQA/Fig/Table/Protocol/Cloning are expert-authored but SeqQA+DbQA (~1,400 of 2,457) are program-generated, so 'synthetic-generated' is a defensible alternative; called expert-authored on the benchmark's headline identity. tool_access=[none] because the eval was explicitly tool-free even though the tasks are designed to be tool-dependent. Headline metric is MC accuracy (exact-match); the open-response rerun grading mechanism is not specified in the text.

title: Can LLM Already Serve as A Database Interface? A BIg Bench for Large-Scale Database Grounded Text-to-SQLs
human_time: A skilled analyst writes one SQL query (avg 30.9 tokens, ~1 JOIN) over a dirty schema in ~5-15 min; simple items faster, challenging (10%) longer.
frontier_score: GPT-4 54.89% execution accuracy vs 92.96% human experts (hidden test, oracle knowledge)
one_line: Large-scale text-to-SQL benchmark: 12,751 questions over 95 dirty real-world databases (33GB) with hand-annotated evidence and the VES efficiency metric.
notes: Text-to-SQL = coding (SQL synthesis); knowledge secondary because external-knowledge grounding (worth ~+20 pts) is the paper's headline bottleneck. Grading is execution-result match against gold = programmatic. tool_access=[none] because the base eval is text (schema+question+evidence)->SQL with no execution feedback to the model; a domain-api reading is possible for agentic variants (DIN-SQL). Provenance is borderline: databases are real-work-mined (Kaggle) but the NL-question/SQL pairs are written by vetted crowds -> called crowdsourced. Saturation=closing: published best is 54.89% (~59% of the 92.96% ceiling, borderline open) but the explainer states agentic pipelines have since closed much of the gap.

title: From Crowdsourced Data to High-Quality Benchmarks: Arena-Hard and BenchBuilder Pipeline
human_time: Answering one hard open-ended prompt (Python/SQL/logic-puzzle style) takes minutes to tens of minutes; single-turn, no ground truth.
one_line: Automated pipeline (BenchBuilder) mining crowd chat logs into Arena-Hard-Auto: 500 hard open-ended prompts, LLM-judged pairwise vs a baseline, tracking Chatbot Arena.
notes: Grades new models via pairwise GPT-4-Turbo judge (5-pt Likert, CoT, no per-item rubric) = llm-judge; the 7 quality indicators are for prompt selection, not answer grading. saturation=not-applicable: it is a Bradley-Terry win-rate leaderboard vs a fixed baseline, no ceiling (frontier_score null). Provenance=user-traffic (prompts mined from live Chatbot Arena/WildChat queries); 'crowdsourced' is a near-synonym here. contamination_defense is the biggest judgment call: the BenchBuilder pipeline enables refresh BY DESIGN (live-refresh) but the released 500-prompt artifact is static/public with no defense, so 'none-public' is defensible for what people download.

title: LLM Defenses Are Not Robust to Multi-Turn Human Jailbreaks Yet
human_time: ~30 min budget per red-team jailbreak attempt (up to 2 red teamers); WMDP-Bio recoveries averaged 20.5 min per success under unlimited time.
frontier_score: multi-turn human ASR 51.7-87.9% vs automated-ensemble upper bound 0-68.3%
one_line: Multi-turn human red-teaming study + MHJ dataset (537 jailbreaks / 2,912 prompts, 7-tactic taxonomy) breaking 4 SOTA LLM defenses on HarmBench.
notes: task_shape=multi-turn: expert red teamers converse across turns (91.6% of breaks needed >1 turn), no tool/environment loop for the model. Grading: HarmBench ASR adjudicated by a GPT-4o classifier (llm-judge) inside a human-review pipeline; WMDP-Bio hand-graded (human) -> grading_all includes both. Provenance=expert-authored (Scale AI red-team conversations are the contribution); the target behaviors themselves are drawn from HarmBench (240) + WMDP-Bio (43), so 'aggregated' is arguable. items_count=537 multi-turn jailbreaks in the released MHJ artifact (2,912 prompts); the ASR eval itself ran on 240 HarmBench behaviors + 43 WMDP-Bio. saturation=open in the sense that defenses are far from robust (high attack success); not a model-capability leaderboard.

title: The WMDP Benchmark: Measuring and Reducing Malicious Use With Unlearning
human_time: One four-choice recognition question of expert precursor knowledge; seconds to about a minute for a domain expert.
frontier_score: Yi-34B base 75.3% on WMDP-Bio (higher = more hazardous knowledge)
one_line: Public 3,668-question four-choice benchmark probing hazardous bio/cyber/chem knowledge as a malicious-use proxy; also ships the RMU unlearning method.
notes: Four-choice MCQ accuracy = exact-match; single-turn, no tools. domain_primary=safety (dangerous-capability proxy) with science (bio/chem) + knowledge (recall) secondary. Provenance=expert-authored (subject experts wrote/curated along the hazard chain), though items derive from public PubMed/GitHub. contamination_defense=none-public (fully public set, no holdout/canary; explainer flags likely pretraining exposure). Saturation semantics are inverted (higher score = more dangerous, not 'solved'): base frontier models reach ~63-75% on Bio -> mapped to closing on the raw 25-100% scale.

title: QuestBench: Can LLMs ask the right question to acquire information in reasoning tasks?
human_time: Identifying the single missing fact in a grade-school-math / logic / blocks-world item takes ~1-5 min; math items are near L1, logic/planning a bit longer.
frontier_score: o1-preview 49.91% Logic-Q, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking 47.32% Planning-Q; up to ~98% on GSME-Q math
one_line: Benchmark testing whether LLMs can name the one missing fact in underspecified reasoning tasks (Logic-Q, Planning-Q, GSM-Q/GSME-Q) framed as 1-sufficient CSPs.
notes: Closed-set multiple-choice (pick a sufficient variable; any sufficient one counts) = exact-match, single-turn. domain_primary=reasoning (logic+planning are the discriminating domains) with math secondary. Provenance is borderline: items reframe prior benchmarks (GSM8K/GSM-Plus, SimpleLogic, BlocksWorld) -> 'aggregated' is arguable, but the underspecified 1-sufficient CSPs are program-constructed (backward search / PDDL / withholding), so called synthetic-generated. contamination_defense=none-public for the released static ~39k-item set; the templated Logic-Q/Planning-Q are procedurally generated (low contamination) while GSM-Q inherits GSM8K exposure, so 'procedural' is defensible. Saturation=open on the hard domains (40-50%) even though math is saturated (~98%). items_count=38,882 (1,150+7,500+23,642+6,590); math evaluated on subsets.

title: ResearchQA: Evaluating Scholarly Question Answering at Scale Across 75 Fields with Survey-Mined Questions and Rubrics
human_time: ~15-30 min for a domain expert to draft a ~250-word cited answer covering ~7.5 rubric items (expert answers in the comparable ScholarQABench average ~289 words); borderline L3 if literature lookup is needed.
frontier_score: sonar-deep-research 75.29% rubric coverage (82% win rate over next-best gemini-2.5-pro); no parametric/retrieval system tops 70%
one_line: Scholarly-QA benchmark of 21.4K survey-mined research questions with 160K per-query rubric items across 75 fields, LLM-judge scored.
notes: Tool access varies by tier: parametric systems run with none, retrieval/deep-research tiers get search; listed the maximal access. Provenance is synthetic (gpt-4.1-mini writes queries/rubrics) but grounded in real survey articles. items_count = 3.7K test split (21.4K total, 16.9K train). Contamination is measured (~1.1% coverage effect) and source surveys blocked from retrieval, but the set is public with no holdout.

title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
human_time: Seconds to ~2 min per item: typical core-scenario instances are one MC/short-QA question (MMLU, BoolQ, HellaSwag); summarization items run a few minutes but are the minority.
frontier_score: text-davinci-002 tops accuracy/robustness/fairness win-rates; e.g. 62.0% TruthfulQA, code-davinci-002 52.1% GSM8K (2022 snapshot)
one_line: The framework that standardized LM eval: 7 metrics x 16 core scenarios (42 total) for 30 models under identical 5-shot prompting.
notes: The eval artifact is an aggregation/reframing of mostly pre-existing datasets (21 of 42 scenarios new to mainstream LM eval, but drawn from prior sources). Saturation call: the explainer treats the v1 rankings as 'historical curiosities' and the constituent scenarios (MMLU, HellaSwag) are community-saturated; HELM lives on as a refreshed framework. Toxicity via PerspectiveAPI classifier binned under proxy-metric.

title: Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context
human_time: ~5-10 min for a human to fully segment one image (avg 7.7 instances/image at >22 worker-hours per 1,000 masks); pure recognition would be seconds, but the artifact is per-instance masks.
frontier_score: In-paper baseline only: DPMv5-P 16.9 AP on COCO vs 29.6 on PASCAL (mAP@[.5:.95] protocol arrived later with the eval server)
one_line: 2.5M instance-segmented objects across 91 categories in 328K cluttered everyday scenes; the decade-defining detection/segmentation benchmark.
notes: Items = 328K images total (2014 test split ~40.8K); grading is IoU-threshold AP against ground-truth masks via the eval server. Saturation from own knowledge (modern detectors ~60-66 box mAP, community still uses it but treats it as aging) - the explainer only quotes 2014 DPM numbers. Test labels withheld behind eval server = private-holdout, though the server postdates the paper.

title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
human_time: ~2 min per question - the paper's own human baseline averaged ~2 minutes on each of 250 questions.
frontier_score: Best 2021 model GPT-3-175B ('helpful' prompt) 58% truthful vs 94% human; the public MC variant is 'now both contaminated and largely saturated'
one_line: 817 adversarial questions targeting imitative falsehoods (popular misconceptions); showed inverse scaling of truthfulness in 2021 models.
notes: Headline generation task is human-graded; ships GPT-judge (finetuned GPT-3-6.7B, 90-96% agreement) as the automated proxy (llm-judge) and a likelihood-based MC variant (exact-match). Saturation refers to the modern public MC usage per the explainer; the original human-graded generation task was open in 2021. Primary domain borderline knowledge vs safety - it is factual QA whose point is a safety-relevant honesty axis.

title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
human_time: Minutes per task: bash/SQL operations, Freebase queries, web shopping, ALFWorld household episodes - 5-50 agent rounds each, each corresponding to a few minutes of human work.
frontier_score: GPT-4 OA 4.01 (relative metric), best on 6 of 8 envs - 78% success House-Holding but 16.6 Lateral Thinking Puzzles, 29.0 Web Browsing
one_line: First multi-environment LLM-as-agent benchmark: 8 execution-graded interactive envs (OS, SQL, KG, games, web) over 5-50 rounds.
notes: Provenance borderline: 5 of 8 environments newly built by the authors (expert-authored), 3 adapted from prior benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Mind2Web) - partly aggregated. items_count = 1,014 test (+269 dev). Headline OA is relative (cohort-normalized), so saturation judged from absolute per-env success rates, most well under 60%. KG answer-F1 has a proxy flavor but grading is env/execution-grounded overall.

title: Is Your Code Generated by ChatGPT Really Correct? Rigorous Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code Generation
human_time: A few minutes per problem - HumanEval-style single Python function synthesis from a docstring.
frontier_score: GPT-4 88.4 pass@1 on HumanEval falls to 76.2 on HumanEval+ (greedy, 2023)
one_line: HumanEval+ hardens HumanEval's 164 problems with ~80x more auto-generated tests, cutting pass@k up to ~29% and flipping rankings.
notes: Task items are the unchanged 164 HumanEval problems (hence aggregated); the contribution - the test suite - is synthetic-generated (ChatGPT seeds + type-aware mutation). Contamination explicitly 'not addressed by design'. Saturation partly own knowledge: explainer's 2023 best is 76.2 (closing then), but modern frontier models exceed 90% on HumanEval+ and the community treats it as solved.

title: ContractEval: Benchmarking LLMs for Clause-Level Legal Risk Identification in Commercial Contracts
human_time: A trained legal assistant answering one clause question over a contract (0.6k-301k chars) takes minutes to ~30 min; span lookup, not drafting.
frontier_score: GPT-4.1 mini F1 0.644 / F2 0.678; best open model Qwen3 8B (thinking) F1 0.540
one_line: Scores 19 LLMs on CUAD's clause-level legal-risk span extraction from real contracts, adding conciseness and 'laziness' lenses to F1.
notes: Provenance is borderline: a reframing of the public CUAD test split (aggregated), whose underlying items are real EDGAR contracts annotated by trained law students. Primary TP rule is binary full-span coverage (exact-match-style); Jaccard token overlap is the proxy-metric lens. Long contracts (>=128k context required) make long-context a genuine secondary.

title: Learn to Explain: Multimodal Reasoning via Thought Chains for Science Question Answering
human_time: Grade 1-12 multiple-choice science items; a competent adult answers most in well under a minute (humans score 88.4%).
frontier_score: few-shot GPT-3 (CoT) 75.17% vs humans 88.40% (gold-explanation upper bound 94.13%)
one_line: 21,208 multimodal grade-school science MC questions, each annotated with a lecture and explanation, as a chain-of-thought testbed.
notes: Saturation call uses own knowledge: post-2023 multimodal models exceed the 88.4% human bar and the community treats it as solved; explainer itself only quotes 2022 numbers (closing at the time). Answer accuracy is exact-match; explanation quality graded by humans and by embedding/n-gram similarity (proxy). Test split is 4,241 of the 21,208.

title: MathVista: Evaluating Mathematical Reasoning of Foundation Models in Visual Contexts
human_time: AMT human baseline did 5 questions in 20 minutes (~4 min/item); visually-grounded math items take a competent human a few minutes each.
frontier_score: GPT-4V 49.9% vs human 60.3% on testmini (at publication); explainer notes newer models have since largely saturated it
one_line: 6,141 visual-math problems from 31 datasets (5 tasks x 7 reasoning types) testing mathematical reasoning over figures, plots, and diagrams.
notes: GPT-4 is used only as an answer EXTRACTOR feeding a normalize-then-exact-match score, so grading stays exact-match, not llm-judge. Provenance: 28 of 31 sources are prior public datasets (12% newly expert-annotated). Contamination defense = withheld test-split labels behind an eval server; underlying public images/questions remain exposed. Saturation per the explainer's own bottom line.

title: MCP-Universe: Benchmarking Large Language Models with Real-World Model Context Protocol Servers
human_time: Typical task (route planning, GitHub issue automation, financial lookup, Blender object) takes a competent human ~5-30 min with the same tools; agents average ~8 tool-call steps.
one_line: 231 hand-built agent tasks over 11 live MCP servers (Maps, GitHub, Finance, Blender, Playwright), graded by execution-based evaluators, no LLM judge.
notes: Contamination call is borderline: no per-eval regeneration, but 57.1% of evaluators auto-refetch live ground truth so time-varying answers can't be memorized by design - closest to live-refresh. Static evaluators compare against hand-collected facts (exact-match flavor inside a programmatic harness). Browser (Playwright) and search servers justify agentic-web secondary; long-context blow-up (~90k tokens by step 16) is a named wall.

title: SpreadsheetBench: Towards Challenging Real World Spreadsheet Manipulation
human_time: Real Excel-forum questions (formulas/VBA over messy sheets); an Excel expert resolves a typical one in ~10-30 min (experts score 71.33%, no per-item time stated).
frontier_score: GPT-4o 18.35% soft / 15.02% hard; Copilot in Excel ~20% (subset); Excel experts 71.33%
one_line: 912 real Excel-forum manipulation requests over messy spreadsheets, graded online-judge style: one code solution must pass 3 input-output test sheets.
notes: Headline setting is one inference emitting code with no tools (tool_access none); a multi-round variant adds code-execution feedback and lifts most models ~10x. Contamination_defense is the closest fit, not exact: construction-time perturbations (GPT-4-rewritten instructions, twice-modified sheets, moved answer positions) defeat memorized forum solutions, but instances are fixed at release, not regenerated per-eval. Domain: work product is office/spreadsheet work but the evaluated act is program synthesis - coding primary, professional secondary.

title: MathDial: A Dialogue Tutoring Dataset with Rich Pedagogical Properties Grounded in Math Reasoning Problems
human_time: One tutoring dialogue over a GSM8K problem runs up to ~10 turns; a teacher spends roughly 5-15 minutes guiding a student through one item.
frontier_score: human tutors Success@10 82% at 4% Telling; ChatGPT 53% success but 32% telling; finetuned Flan-T5-780M 39% at 4%
one_line: 2,861 teacher-LLM-student math-tutoring dialogues with per-turn scaffolding moves; Success@k vs Telling@k makes guide-vs-tell measurable.
notes: Grading_primary is a judgment call: the signature Success@k/Telling@k metrics are computed programmatically via interactive simulation against an LLM student (itself a soft grader - circularity flagged by the explainer); sBLEU/BERTScore are proxy, plus a small human eval. Domain: the tested skill is pedagogical restraint/scaffolding (social-moral per rubric), grounded in math dialogue. Provenance: dialogues authored by 91 recruited expert teachers with a synthetic LLM student, over crowdsourced GSM8K problems.

title: Unifying AI Tutor Evaluation: An Evaluation Taxonomy for Pedagogical Ability Assessment of LLM-Powered AI Tutors
human_time: Read a short math-tutoring dialogue and write a one-sentence remediation turn — a human tutor needs a few minutes per instance.
frontier_score: Llama-3.1-405B most pedagogically complete (guidance 77.08%, coherence 91.67%); GPT-4 reveals the answer ~47% of the time
one_line: 192 math mistake-remediation dialogues scoring LLM tutors on eight human-annotated pedagogical dimensions (DAMR).
notes: Primary domain is social-moral per the tutoring/pedagogy-as-interpersonal rule, though several dimensions (mistake ID/location) test math understanding. Task is single-turn: one next-tutor-response given a fixed dialogue history, not live multi-turn interaction. Provenance is aggregated (dialogues from Bridge and MathDial) with newly generated LLM responses and new human annotations. LLM judges (Prometheus2, Llama-3.1-8B) ship in the eval but correlate negatively with humans, so grading is effectively human-only. Saturation is dimension-dependent (human-likeness ~96% vs tone in the teens).

title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
human_time: One attack prompt yields one 512-token completion; a human writing an attempted harmful how-to of that length would take a few minutes.
frontier_score: Zephyr + R2D2 cuts GCG ASR from 69.5% to 5.5%; no attack or defense is uniformly effective across 18 attacks x 33 models
one_line: 510 curated harmful behaviors in a fixed attack->completion->classifier pipeline standardizing automated red-teaming comparison.
notes: Grading is a fine-tuned Llama-2-13B harm classifier (93.2% human agreement) — classified as llm-judge; copyright behaviors use a hash-based programmatic check. Saturation marked not-applicable: the metric is attack-success-rate over attack/defense pairs, with no single frontier score to saturate. Canonical val/test split defends method tuning, not training contamination — behaviors are fully public.

title: Remote Labor Index: Measuring AI Automation of Remote Work
human_time: Paper's own numbers: mean 28.9 h, median 11.5 h of professional work per project (max 450 h) — one to several working days.
frontier_score: Manus, 2.5% automation rate ($1,720 of $143,991); Elo 509.9 vs human baseline 1,000
one_line: 240 real Upwork freelance projects (video, CAD, design, games, audio...) graded by holistic human review against the paid human deliverable.
notes: Tool access varies by agent product (CLI vs computer-use scaffolds; Manus/ChatGPT agent browse) — listed the union. ~80% of the work is non-software, hence professional primary with coding/multimodal secondary. Grading tried per-project rubrics and explicitly rejected them for holistic client-acceptance judgment (94.4% agreement). 230 of 240 projects are a private holdout.

title: Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces
human_time: Tasks like COBOL->Python reimplementation, FEAL differential cryptanalysis, or a path tracer plausibly take an expert 1 h to a day; agents grind up to 2 h. Author-predicted difficulty, not measured human time.
frontier_score: GPT-5.2 via Codex CLI, 63% resolution rate; best open-weight (Kimi K2 Thinking) 36%
one_line: 89 hard containerized terminal tasks (SWE, sysadmin, security, scientific computing) graded by hidden tests on the final container state.
notes: Terminal agent doing software-flavored work -> coding primary per rubric, agentic-web secondary. Agents get live internet access through the terminal (folded into code-exec). Provenance: 93 community contributors wrote 229 tasks, heavily expert-audited down to 89 (~3 reviewer-hours/task) — crowdsourced with expert curation. Public repo + internet during eval; strongest shipped defense is a BIG-Bench canary string. Failure-mode taxonomies use LLM judges but do not grade the benchmark score. Authors themselves forecast saturation within a year.

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
human_time: Writing a paragraph-length biography of a named person takes a human a few minutes (bios average 26-41 atomic facts).
frontier_score: Search-augmented PerplexityAI 71.5% FActScore (human eval); ChatGPT 58.3%, InstructGPT 42.5%
one_line: Scores long-form factuality as the fraction of a generation's atomic facts supported by Wikipedia, with an automatic estimator within ~2% of humans.
notes: Eval-methodology paper; classified the shipped artifact (the people-biographies FActScore eval). Grading call is borderline: the automated estimator decomposes into atomic facts (InstructGPT) and validates each with retrieval+LM True/False — per-fact LLM verification filed as llm-judge; human annotation is the gold variant and the nonparametric masked-LM likelihood in the ensemble is a proxy-metric. Provenance: prompts are program-generated from 183 Wikidata entities sampled across rarity buckets. Standard subjects are tool-free LMs (PerplexityAI's search is a property of one evaluated system, not the benchmark). items_count = 183 human-eval entities; the unlabeled scaled study uses 500 prompts/model.

title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?
human_time: Market payouts average ~$670/task (range $250-$32k); at freelance rates a typical Expensify bug fix is several hours to about a day.
frontier_score: Claude 3.5 Sonnet earns $403,325 of $1M (26.2% IC SWE pass@1 on Diamond); IC SWE pass@1 under 30% for all models
one_line: 1,488 real $1M-payout Upwork/Expensify freelance jobs: agent coding graded by human-written end-to-end Playwright tests plus manager proposal-picking.
notes: Per rubric, code-for-pay -> coding primary with professional secondary. IC SWE (the capability signal) is graded programmatically by triple-verified E2E browser tests; Manager tasks (724 of 1,488, $585k) are effectively multiple choice against the real hiring decision — exact-match. The 'user tool' (a Playwright script the agent invokes to see its work) is filed as domain-api; the Docker sandbox has no internet. $499,200 of tasks are a private holdout.

title: ToolComp: A Multi-Tool Reasoning & Process Supervision Benchmark
human_time: Each prompt chains several dependent tool lookups/calculations (search, weather, stocks, Python); a person with the same tools would need roughly 5-20 minutes per item.
frontier_score: o1-preview 61.83% final-answer accuracy (benchmark average 46.64%)
one_line: 485 held-out multi-tool ReAct tasks with human-verified answers plus 1,731 per-step process-supervision labels.
notes: Prompts are model-generated few-shot then human-filtered and hand-refined, so provenance is borderline expert-authored; chose synthetic-generated. 61.83% frontier sits right at the open/closing boundary on a stale Jan-2025 leaderboard. Final-answer grading is GPT-4-Turbo judge vs a ground-truth answer (exact-match cross-check in appendix).

title: MLGym: A New Framework and Benchmark for Advancing AI Research Agents
human_time: Improving a provided non-SOTA baseline on a task like CIFAR-10 or MNLI takes an ML practitioner a few hours of iterative coding and training; agent runs are capped at 50 steps of up to 30 min each.
frontier_score: o1-preview Best Attempt AUP@4 = 1.150 (Best Submission 1.176)
one_line: Gymnasium framework + 13 open-ended ML-research tasks where agents improve baselines, scored by cross-task AUP.
notes: AUP is normalized against the best model in the pool, so the aggregate is relative with no ceiling -> saturation not-applicable (per-task metrics do have ceilings). Provenance = aggregated: all 13 tasks reframe canonical public datasets/benchmarks (CIFAR-10, MNLI, Atari), which is also the contamination weakness. BLEU on MS-COCO captioning is the proxy-metric component.

title: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
human_time: Selecting and writing one correct fully-qualified ML-hub API call is a short documentation lookup, roughly 1-5 minutes for a practitioner.
frontier_score: Gorilla-7B zero-shot AST accuracy: 59.13 TorchHub / 71.68 HuggingFace / 83.79 TensorHub
one_line: APIBench: 1,645 ML-hub APIs with GPT-4-generated instructions, grading API-call correctness via AST sub-tree matching.
notes: tool_access is mode-dependent: the headline zero-shot setting is pure text, but the benchmark's retrieval settings feed a BM25/GPT-Index-retrieved doc; listed search-retrieval. AST sub-tree matching is a syntactic checker, not execution. items_count = 16,450 total instruction-API pairs; the eval split size is not stated. APIs scraped from real hubs but the eval items themselves are Self-Instruct synthetic.

title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
human_time: Paper states tasks average ~7 hours (404 min) of expert effort, ranging up to multiple weeks -- the rubric's canonical L4 example.
frontier_score: Claude Opus 4.1: 47.6% wins+ties vs the human expert's own deliverable (50% = parity)
one_line: 1,320 real knowledge-work tasks in 44 occupations, graded by blinded expert pairwise comparison against human work.
notes: Models ran under vendor-specific tool scaffolds (web search, code interpreter, file creation UI) but the task itself is one-shot fully-specified request -> deliverable with no environment feedback loop mandated, so single-turn. Win-rate-vs-human has no fixed ceiling by design; scored open with best model at 47.6% vs the 50% parity line. Tasks authored by recruited experts from their real work product -- borderline real-work-mined.

title: Humanity's Last Exam
human_time: Closed-ended frontier academic exam questions; a matched domain expert typically needs minutes to tens of minutes per item (math derivations at the long end).
frontier_score: o3-mini (high) 13.4% at release (text-only subset); o1 8.0% on the full set
one_line: 2,500 expert-written closed-ended academic questions kept only if frontier models fail them; 41% mathematics.
notes: 24% MC + 76% exact-match short answers, with an o3-mini judge used only for answer-equivalence calls -> exact-match primary, llm-judge secondary. Questions must not be recoverable by quick web search, and models are evaluated without tools. Explainer notes public-set scores climbed fast post-release; the 13.4% frontier number is on the easier text-only slice.

title: LegalBench-RAG: A Benchmark for Retrieval-Augmented Generation in the Legal Domain
human_time: Locating the exact clause answering a query inside a contract (MAUD docs average 351K chars) takes a lawyer minutes to tens of minutes with document search.
frontier_score: Best config (RCTS, no reranker): 6.41% mean Precision@1 / 62.22% Recall@64; MAUD hardest at 28.28% Recall@64
one_line: 6,889 expert-traced query-to-exact-span retrieval pairs over ~80M chars of legal documents; scores the retrieval step.
notes: Evaluates retrieval pipelines (embedder/chunker/reranker), not LLMs directly. Span-overlap Precision/Recall@k is computed against gold character spans but never formally defined in the paper; classified programmatic rather than proxy-metric. Provenance aggregated: retraces LegalBench/CUAD/ContractNLI/MAUD/PrivacyQA expert annotations back to source spans (inherited expert-authored labels).

title: DEBAGREEMENT: A comment-reply dataset for (dis)agreement detection in online debates
human_time: One comment-reply pair labeled agree/neutral/disagree; annotators judge a pair in well under a minute.
frontier_score: RoBERTa-large and DeBERTa-large (fine-tuned) tie at 64.1% accuracy; all nine 2021 encoders land in 62-64%
one_line: 42,894 real Reddit comment-reply pairs labeled agree/neutral/disagree, with a temporal user-interaction graph attached.
notes: Items mined from real Reddit threads but labels are crowdsourced (529 Scale AI workers) - real-work-mined chosen for the items themselves. social-moral secondary is borderline (reading sarcasm/partial agreement is social signal). 'Closing' at 64% is soft: only 33% unanimous labels, so the true ceiling is below 100% and no frontier LLM was ever scored.

title: LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
human_time: Variants of TheAgentCompany, SWE-Bench Pro, and MCP-Atlas tasks - multi-stage workflows of dozens-to-hundreds of actions; a competent human would need roughly 30 min to several hours per instance.
frontier_score: Opus-4.5 on MCP-Atlas: 100% base -> 47% underspecified -> 78% pass@3 with ask_user; GPT-5.2 SWE-Bench Pro recovers to 91%
one_line: 285 controllably underspecified variants of long-horizon agent tasks that test whether agents notice missing info and ask well.
notes: Cross-domain meta-benchmark: tasks span office/agentic work (85 TAC), coding (100 SWE-Bench Pro), and MCP tool-use (100) - agentic-web primary is a judgment call. Provenance is an LLM perturbation pipeline over prior benchmarks (synthetic over aggregated base). 'Procedural' defense reflects the regenerable pipeline; the shipped 285 set itself is static. Browser access in TAC subset unstated in text.

title: SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution
human_time: Codebase Q&A / test-writing / refactoring in 18 real repos, requiring running the app and multi-file edits; a competent engineer would need roughly 1-4 hours per task (refactors anchor to real merged PRs up to thousands of lines).
frontier_score: GPT-5.4 (Codex) 43.49% Pass@1, Opus 4.7 (Claude Code) 41.89%; Pass3 drops to ~29% for both
one_line: 284 expert-written tasks grading coding agents on codebase Q&A, test writing, and refactoring quality, not just passing tests.
notes: Pass requires functional gate AND all must-have rubrics; rubric-judge chosen as primary since the rubric is the distinguishing gate and the Q&A split (124 tasks) is rubric-only. Ships a memorization/leakage audit (Jaccard screen, tool-call audit) but no held-out or refreshed set, so defense = none-public.

title: SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension of Text
human_time: Read one Wikipedia paragraph and highlight an answer span - tens of seconds per question for a human.
frontier_score: Human 86.8% F1 / 77.0% EM; neural models hit 70.3% F1 within four months and the human gap was 'erased entirely by 2018'
one_line: 107,785 crowdsourced questions over Wikipedia where every answer is a passage span, gradable by exact-match and token F1.
notes: Headline metric is token F1 (proxy-metric per rubric) but EM is a co-headline and the span design is 'exact-match gradable' - borderline primary call. Private-holdout is from own knowledge (official hidden test set via Codalab leaderboard), not stated in the text; the explainer now calls it fully contaminated/saturated.

title: AndroidWorld: A Dynamic Benchmarking Environment for Autonomous Agents
human_time: Humans succeed 80% and agents average 3.9 min per task at ~3x human speed, so roughly 1-2 minutes per task for a human; most tasks are a handful of UI steps, tail 30+.
frontier_score: M3A (GPT-4 Turbo, accessibility-tree input) 30.6% vs 80.0% human; 3-seed mean 29.0%
one_line: Android emulator with 116 tasks across 20 real apps, graded by device system state and re-parameterized per random seed.
notes: Also ships 92 ported MobileMiniWoB++ web tasks on the same interface (not counted in items_count). Procedural defense = seeded task parameterization generating fresh goals/success criteria per run; templates and apps are still public. Mobile GUI control mapped to computer-os; domain-api for the send_text_message-style function-calling APIs. 30.6% is a 2024 single-seed number on zero-shot scaffolds.

title: Do ImageNet Classifiers Generalize to ImageNet?
human_time: Single-image classification into one of the ImageNet/CIFAR-10 classes - a few seconds per image for a human.
frontier_score: Best 2018 model (PNASNet) 82.9% orig -> 72.2% top-1 on ImageNetV2 MatchedFrequency (an 10.7pp drop)
one_line: ImageNetV2 and CIFAR-10.1: from-scratch replications of the classic test sets that reveal an 11-14pp accuracy drop.
notes: Methodology paper; classified artifact is the replicated test sets (10,000 ImageNetV2 + 2,000 CIFAR-10.1 = 12,000). Items scraped from Flickr/Tiny Images then MTurk-filtered - crowdsourced chosen since worker selection frequency literally defines the item set. Fresh-at-release but public and static thereafter, hence none-public. Saturation judged from the explainer's 72.2% top-1; the paper is a measurement study, not a leaderboard.

title: GPQA: A Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A Benchmark
human_time: One graduate-level MC science question; non-experts spent median 30 min with web, in-domain experts far less — a single hard multiple-choice item.
frontier_score: GPT-4 + search: 39.4% (Extended) at 2023 release; experts 65%, non-experts 34%, chance 25%
one_line: 448 graduate-level bio/physics/chemistry multiple-choice questions written by PhDs to be objective for experts yet Google-proof for non-experts.
notes: Canonical use is closed-book (none); paper also ran a GPT-4 + search-retrieval condition, not reflected in tool_access. Saturation marked 'closing' per the explainer's 'closing in on saturation' language for Diamond; external knowledge suggests Diamond is now near-saturated for frontier reasoning models, but the text only quotes 2023 numbers. Main-set count used (448); Extended is 546, Diamond 198.

title: The MASK Benchmark: Disentangling Honesty From Accuracy in AI Systems
human_time: Reading a hand-crafted pressure scenario and answering a short factual (binary/numerical) proposition — roughly 1-5 min per item.
frontier_score: Most honest Claude 3.7 Sonnet P(Lie)=26.6%; worst Grok 2 63.0%; no model explicitly honest in >46% of cases
one_line: 1,500 pressure-scenario examples measuring whether an LLM knowingly lies (states what it doesn't believe), scored separately from factual accuracy.
notes: Grading: an LLM judge (86.4% human agreement) maps statement S and elicited belief B to proposition resolutions, then a lie is scored programmatically as S≠B. task_shape marked single-turn (belief elicitation is separate queries, not a dialogue), though 'Doubling Down'/'Continuations' archetypes are conversational. items_count is 1000 public + 500 held-out; paper reports on the combined 1500. Accuracy axis is genuinely measured, hence knowledge secondary.

title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
human_time: Labeling one image (classify among 1000 classes) takes seconds; the trained human annotator did 1500 images after a short training phase.
frontier_score: GoogLeNet 6.7% top-5 error (2014); trained human A1 5.1% on the same sample
one_line: 1000-class ImageNet image-classification challenge (plus single-object localization and 200-class detection), scored by top-5 error on a withheld test set.
notes: Classification top-5 error is label exact-match; localization/detection use box-IoU/mAP (programmatic), hence grading_all includes programmatic. Provenance crowdsourced (web images labeled via Amazon Mechanical Turk with QC voting). items_count ≈ 100,000 test images; training set is ~1.2M. Treated as saturated: 2014 machine error already near the single-human baseline and long since surpassed.

title: PropensityBench: Evaluating Latent Safety Risks in Large Language Models via an Agentic Approach
human_time: The underlying task τ is a short multi-step tool operation; a scenario runs up to 156 agent interactions across 12 pressure levels, but the equivalent human work per instance is minutes (synthetic agentic task).
frontier_score: Avg propensity 46.9% at max pressure; safest OpenAI o3 10.5%, worst Gemini 2.5 Pro 79.0% (near-zero at zero pressure)
one_line: 5,874 agentic scenarios that rig the safe tool to always fail and escalate operational pressure, measuring an LLM's propensity to grab a forbidden dangerous-capability tool.
notes: Headline propensity is a mechanical/programmatic check (did the agent ever call the misaligned tool?); LLM judges are used in scenario generation and the justification taxonomy (hence llm-judge in grading_all). Contamination: scenarios are freshly synthesized and the metric is behavioral (low exposure), but no explicit held-out/canary/live-refresh mechanism is described and templated pressure prompts are Goodhart-able, so marked none-public — low confidence on this dimension. Saturation 'open' interpreted as the safety target (near-zero propensity) being far from met; could be argued not-applicable since it's a lower-is-better propensity metric. Complexity is a judgment call on synthetic agentic tasks.

title: SciPredict: Can LLMs Predict the Outcomes of Scientific Experiments in Natural Sciences?
human_time: Reading one fully-specified experimental setup and predicting its outcome — an expert reasoning task of roughly minutes to tens of minutes per item.
